question: A patient cannot identify objects placed in their left palm without looking, yet basic touch and vibration are preserved. Which cortical region is most likely affected?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize symptoms of cortical sensory loss (tactile agnosia, agraphesthesia, extinction), upper motor neuron injury, apraxia (ideomotor & ideational), and forms of hemineglect. Associate these presentations with locations of cortical injury or dysfunction.

options.text: Lesion of right superior parietal lobule
options.rationale: Correct because tactile object recognition deficits (astereognosis) with preserved basic sensation localize to the superior parietal lobule as described in the lecture.

options.text: Lesion of left primary somatosensory cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because primary somatosensory lesions produce loss of basic discrimination rather than isolated inability to recognize objects; if two-point discrimination were lost, this would be more likely.

options.text: Lesion of right secondary somatosensory operculum
options.rationale: Wrong because S2 has bilateral receptive fields and integrates salience; isolated unilateral tactile agnosia without bilateral findings makes S2 less likely; bilateral S2 dysfunction would better explain bilateral deficits.

options.text: Lesion of left frontal premotor cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because premotor damage causes planning deficits (apraxia) rather than pure tactile object recognition loss; if motor planning for object manipulation were impaired, this would be plausible.

options.text: Lesion of right thalamic VPL nucleus
options.rationale: Wrong because thalamic lesions typically cause more global sensory loss including pain and temperature; if pinprick and vibration were absent, thalamic injury would be more consistent.

question: During an exam a patient cannot recognize numbers traced on the right palm but reports touch normally. Which cortical area most likely explains this deficit?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize symptoms of cortical sensory loss (tactile agnosia, agraphesthesia, extinction), upper motor neuron injury, apraxia (ideomotor & ideational), and forms of hemineglect. Associate these presentations with locations of cortical injury or dysfunction.

options.text: Lesion of left superior parietal lobule
options.rationale: Correct because inability to identify characters drawn on skin (agraphesthesia) with intact basic sensation localizes to somatosensory association cortex in the superior parietal lobule.

options.text: Lesion of right primary somatosensory cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because primary cortex damage usually impairs basic sensation; if pinprick and vibration were reduced, primary cortex would be implicated.

options.text: Lesion of left secondary somatosensory cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because S2 involvement often affects bilateral receptive fields and attention; isolated agraphesthesia on one side is more typical of superior parietal damage.

options.text: Lesion of left frontal supplementary motor area
options.rationale: Wrong because supplementary motor lesions impair self-initiated movement sequences rather than tactile recognition; if motor sequencing were abnormal, SMA would be considered.

options.text: Lesion of right posterior parietal cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because right posterior parietal lesions more commonly produce spatial neglect; if the patient ignored stimuli on the right, this would be more consistent.

question: A patient has contralateral weakness predominantly of the lower limb with sparing of the face and arm. Which arterial territory is most consistent with this motor pattern?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the vascular supply to the primary motor and somatosensory cortices.

options.text: Anterior cerebral artery territory infarct
options.rationale: Correct because ACA supplies medial precentral regions controlling lower extremity motor function, producing contralateral leg weakness with relative face and arm sparing.

options.text: Middle cerebral artery superior division infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because MCA superior division typically affects face and arm motor areas; if face and arm were weak, MCA superior division would be likely.

options.text: Posterior cerebral artery cortical infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because PCA supplies occipital and inferior temporal cortex; if visual field defects predominated, PCA would be more plausible.

options.text: Watershed ACA-MCA borderzone infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because watershed lesions often cause proximal arm and trunk weakness (man-in-the-barrel); if proximal upper limb weakness were present, watershed infarct would fit better.

options.text: Lacunar internal capsule infarct sparing cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because lacunar capsular strokes produce pure motor hemiparesis affecting face and arm as well; if face and arm were involved, lacune would be considered.

question: A patient with a focal destructive lesion in the left frontal lobe has eyes deviated toward the lesion side. How is this gaze preference best described?
cited_learning_objective: Differentiate “right-way “ and “wrong-way” eyes; recall them by the types of lesion that produce them (destructive vs. stimulating) and where those lesions are occurring (cerebral cortex vs. brainstem)

options.text: Ipsilesional gaze preference due to cortical destruction
options.rationale: Correct because destructive cortical lesions produce gaze deviation toward the side of the lesion (right-way eyes) as the intact contralateral frontal eye field drives gaze toward the lesion side.

options.text: Contralesional gaze preference from cortical stimulation
options.rationale: Wrong because cortical stimulation (eg seizure) causes eyes to look away from the lesion; if the patient had a seizure, contralesional deviation would be expected.

options.text: Ipsilesional gaze preference from pontine lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because brainstem lesions often produce gaze palsies rather than ipsilesional preference; if pontine gaze center were damaged, inability to look toward lesion would occur.

options.text: Contralesional gaze preference from cerebellar dysfunction
options.rationale: Wrong because cerebellar lesions cause coordination deficits and nystagmus rather than sustained conjugate gaze deviation; if dysmetria and ataxia predominated, cerebellar cause would be likely.

options.text: Ipsilesional gaze preference from bilateral frontal damage
options.rationale: Wrong because bilateral frontal lesions produce diffuse impairment of voluntary gaze rather than a unilateral preference; if both frontal eye fields were destroyed, gaze would be globally impaired.

question: A patient demonstrates slowed voluntary movements, increased tone, and an extensor plantar response on the right. Which cortical area lesion best explains these findings?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize symptoms of cortical sensory loss (tactile agnosia, agraphesthesia, extinction), upper motor neuron injury, apraxia (ideomotor & ideational), and forms of hemineglect. Associate these presentations with locations of cortical injury or dysfunction.

options.text: Lesion of left primary motor cortex
options.rationale: Correct because UMN signs contralateral to a precentral gyrus lesion (weakness, hyperreflexia, increased tone, Babinski) are classic for primary motor cortex damage.

options.text: Lesion of left premotor cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because premotor lesions cause planning deficits and apraxia rather than prominent UMN signs; if motor weakness without hyperreflexia were present, premotor involvement would be more likely.

options.text: Lesion of right primary somatosensory cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because somatosensory cortex lesions produce sensory discrimination deficits rather than UMN signs; if sensory extinction and agraphesthesia were present, somatosensory cortex would be implicated.

options.text: Lesion of left cerebellar hemisphere
options.rationale: Wrong because cerebellar lesions cause hypotonia and ataxia rather than increased tone and extensor plantar responses; if dysmetria and intention tremor were present, cerebellar cause would fit.

options.text: Lesion of right internal capsule lacune
options.rationale: Wrong because a right capsular lacune would produce left-sided UMN signs; if contralateral (left) weakness were observed, internal capsule infarct would be considered.

question: A patient cannot perform a familiar sequence of dressing despite intact comprehension and limb strength. Which cortical region is most likely dysfunctional?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize symptoms of cortical sensory loss (tactile agnosia, agraphesthesia, extinction), upper motor neuron injury, apraxia (ideomotor & ideational), and forms of hemineglect. Associate these presentations with locations of cortical injury or dysfunction.

options.text: Lesion of dominant parietal association cortex
options.rationale: Correct because ideational apraxia and impaired multistep task sequencing localize to parietal association regions that integrate sensory input into motor plans.

options.text: Lesion of primary motor cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because primary motor lesions cause weakness and UMN signs; if strength were reduced, M1 would be implicated rather than apraxia.

options.text: Lesion of primary somatosensory cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because S1 lesions impair discrimination and position sense; if tactile discrimination were absent, S1 would be more likely.

options.text: Lesion of supplementary motor area
options.rationale: Wrong because SMA lesions impair self-initiated sequences but often spare conceptual sequencing; if initiation of movement sequences from memory were specifically lost, SMA would be implicated.

options.text: Lesion of right inferior parietal lobule
options.rationale: Wrong because right inferior parietal damage more commonly causes spatial neglect; if the patient ignored the left side of clothing, right IPL would be more likely.

question: A stroke produces weakness of the contralateral face and arm but spares the leg. Which cortical artery is most likely occluded?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the vascular supply to the primary motor and somatosensory cortices.

options.text: Middle cerebral artery cortical branch occlusion
options.rationale: Correct because MCA supplies lateral convexity including face and upper limb motor areas, producing contralateral faciobrachial paresis with leg sparing.

options.text: Anterior cerebral artery cortical branch occlusion
options.rationale: Wrong because ACA supplies medial leg area; if leg weakness predominated with arm sparing, ACA would be the likely culprit.

options.text: Posterior cerebral artery cortical branch occlusion
options.rationale: Wrong because PCA infarcts cause visual and inferior temporal deficits; if visual field loss were present, PCA would be considered.

options.text: Watershed ACA-MCA borderzone infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because watershed infarcts classically cause proximal arm and trunk weakness (man-in-the-barrel); if proximal arm weakness were bilateral, watershed would fit better.

options.text: Lacunar infarct of internal capsule
options.rationale: Wrong because lacunar capsular strokes often cause pure motor hemiparesis including face and arm; if cortical signs like aphasia or neglect were absent, lacune would be more likely.

question: A patient with a right parietal association lesion ignores stimuli on the left side when both sides are touched simultaneously. Which phenomenon explains this?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize symptoms of cortical sensory loss (tactile agnosia, agraphesthesia, extinction), upper motor neuron injury, apraxia (ideomotor & ideational), and forms of hemineglect. Associate these presentations with locations of cortical injury or dysfunction.

options.text: Extinction on double simultaneous stimulation
options.rationale: Correct because extinction—failure to perceive contralateral stimuli during bilateral stimulation—is characteristic of parietal association cortex lesions causing hemineglect.

options.text: Primary sensory loss from contralateral cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because primary sensory loss would reduce detection even with unilateral stimulation; if basic sensation were absent on the left, primary cortex would be implicated.

options.text: Peripheral neuropathy causing asymmetric sensation
options.rationale: Wrong because peripheral neuropathy affects peripheral nerve territories and would not selectively extinguish contralateral stimuli only during bilateral testing; if distal sensory loss were present, neuropathy would be considered.

options.text: Motor-intentional neglect preventing left movement
options.rationale: Wrong because motor-intentional neglect affects voluntary movement initiation rather than tactile perception; if the patient could not move the left limb despite feeling touch, motor-intentional neglect would be likely.

options.text: Allesthesia with mislocalization to right side
options.rationale: Wrong because allesthesia involves misreporting stimulus location to the opposite side; if the patient reported left stimuli as right, allesthesia would be the correct term.

question: Microstimulation of a monkey cortical site elicits coordinated hand-to-mouth movements and encodes action goals rather than single muscles. Which human cortical area is analogous?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the anatomical location, Brodmann numbers (where indicated), interconnections, and function of somatosensory and motor cortical areas: primary somatosensory, somatosensory association, primary motor, premotor, supplementary motor, parietal association

options.text: Primary motor cortex on precentral gyrus
options.rationale: Correct because M1 contains neurons (including Betz cells) that encode action goals for body segments and contribute substantially to corticospinal output, as described in the lecture.

options.text: Primary somatosensory cortex on postcentral gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because S1 encodes sensory receptive fields rather than motor action goals; if stimulation produced sensory percepts rather than movements, S1 would be implicated.

options.text: Supplementary motor area on superior frontal gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because SMA is involved in self-initiated complex sequences rather than direct encoding of segmental action goals; if movement sequences from memory were evoked, SMA would be more likely.

options.text: Premotor cortex on lateral frontal gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because premotor cortex selects movements based on external cues and provides corticospinal fibers but is less directly tied to Betz-cell mediated distal control; if cue-driven movement selection were observed, premotor cortex would fit.

options.text: Secondary somatosensory operculum cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because S2 integrates somatosensory information and has bilateral receptive fields rather than producing coordinated motor outputs; if bilateral tactile responses changed with attention, S2 would be implicated.

question: A patient cannot initiate a remembered sequence of finger movements but can perform the same sequence when visually cued. Which cortical area is most likely impaired?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the anatomical location, Brodmann numbers (where indicated), interconnections, and function of somatosensory and motor cortical areas: primary somatosensory, somatosensory association, primary motor, premotor, supplementary motor, parietal association

options.text: Supplementary motor area on superior frontal gyrus
options.rationale: Correct because SMA is active during self-initiated and memorized motor sequences and its dysfunction impairs internally generated movements while sparing externally cued actions.

options.text: Premotor cortex on lateral frontal gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because premotor cortex is involved in externally cued movement selection; if the deficit were limited to cue-driven tasks, premotor cortex would be implicated.

options.text: Primary motor cortex on precentral gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because M1 lesions produce weakness and UMN signs rather than selective inability to self-initiate sequences; if strength were reduced, M1 would be more likely.

options.text: Right parietal association cortex
options.rationale: Wrong because parietal association lesions cause spatial and attentional deficits rather than isolated failure of self-initiated motor sequences; if spatial neglect accompanied the problem, parietal cortex would be considered.

options.text: Primary somatosensory cortex on postcentral gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because S1 lesions impair sensory discrimination rather than initiation of remembered motor sequences; if tactile feedback were absent, S1 would be implicated.

question: A patient with a left frontal seizure has eyes deviated to the right during the event. Which mechanism best explains this transient gaze deviation?
cited_learning_objective: Differentiate “right-way “ and “wrong-way” eyes; recall them by the types of lesion that produce them (destructive vs. stimulating) and where those lesions are occurring (cerebral cortex vs. brainstem)

options.text: Irritative stimulation of left frontal eye field driving right gaze
options.rationale: Correct because cortical stimulation (eg seizure) of the frontal eye field causes conjugate gaze toward the contralateral side (wrong-way eyes), matching the lecture description.

options.text: Destructive lesion of left frontal eye field causing right gaze
options.rationale: Wrong because destructive lesions produce gaze toward the lesion (right-way) rather than away; if the lesion were destructive rather than stimulatory, gaze would be toward the lesion.

options.text: Right pontine gaze center stimulation causing right gaze
options.rationale: Wrong because pontine gaze center lesions typically impair horizontal gaze toward the lesion; if brainstem stimulation produced the deviation, associated cranial nerve deficits would be expected.

options.text: Left cerebellar hemisphere dysfunction causing right gaze
options.rationale: Wrong because cerebellar dysfunction causes dysmetria and nystagmus rather than sustained conjugate gaze deviation; if coordination and balance were primarily affected, cerebellum would be implicated.

options.text: Bilateral frontal eye field suppression causing right gaze
options.rationale: Wrong because bilateral suppression would produce global gaze impairment rather than a unilateral deviation; if both FEFs were suppressed, voluntary gaze would be broadly impaired.

question: A patient has weakness of proximal upper limbs bilaterally after a watershed infarct between ACA and MCA. Which clinical description best fits this syndrome?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize symptoms of cortical sensory loss (tactile agnosia, agraphesthesia, extinction), upper motor neuron injury, apraxia (ideomotor & ideational), and forms of hemineglect. Associate these presentations with locations of cortical injury or dysfunction.

options.text: Man in the barrel syndrome with proximal arm weakness
options.rationale: Correct because ACA-MCA watershed infarcts affecting trunk and proximal arm motor areas produce the characteristic 'man in the barrel' proximal weakness described in the lecture.

options.text: Pure lower extremity monoparesis with leg sensory loss
options.rationale: Wrong because pure leg monoparesis suggests isolated ACA territory infarct of medial motor cortex; if only the leg were weak, ACA cortical infarct would be more likely.

options.text: Faciobrachial paresis sparing proximal arm muscles
options.rationale: Wrong because faciobrachial paresis is typical of MCA cortical strokes affecting face and distal arm; if face weakness were present, MCA infarct would be implicated.

options.text: Bilateral cerebellar ataxia without weakness
options.rationale: Wrong because cerebellar lesions cause coordination deficits rather than proximal weakness; if ataxia and dysmetria predominated, cerebellar cause would fit.

options.text: Peripheral neuropathy causing symmetric proximal weakness
options.rationale: Wrong because peripheral neuropathy usually causes distal symmetric weakness and sensory loss; if distal sensory deficits and stocking distribution were present, neuropathy would be considered.

question: A patient with a left lateral precentral gyrus lesion can raise eyebrows but has lower facial droop on the right. Which tract or cortical representation explains this pattern?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the anatomical location, Brodmann numbers (where indicated), interconnections, and function of somatosensory and motor cortical areas: primary somatosensory, somatosensory association, primary motor, premotor, supplementary motor, parietal association

options.text: Bilateral corticobulbar innervation of forehead muscles
options.rationale: Correct because upper facial muscles receive bilateral corticobulbar input, preserving eyebrow elevation despite contralateral lower facial weakness from lateral precentral (corticobulbar) involvement.

options.text: Unilateral corticospinal lesion sparing facial nucleus
options.rationale: Wrong because corticospinal lesions affect limb motor pathways not facial musculature; if limb weakness without facial asymmetry were present, corticospinal lesion would be implicated.

options.text: Peripheral facial nerve palsy causing lower face droop
options.rationale: Wrong because peripheral CN VII palsy typically affects both upper and lower face on the same side; if eyebrow movement were lost ipsilaterally, peripheral facial nerve lesion would be likely.

options.text: Bilateral cortical lesion causing isolated lower face weakness
options.rationale: Wrong because bilateral cortical lesions would produce more global deficits; if both sides of the face were weak, bilateral cortical damage would be considered.

options.text: Lesion of left cerebellar hemisphere affecting facial tone
options.rationale: Wrong because cerebellar lesions do not produce isolated lower facial weakness; if ataxia and dysmetria accompanied facial changes, cerebellar involvement would be plausible.

question: A patient has bilateral receptive fields in a cortical area that mirrors the primary somatosensory map and projects to limbic structures. Which area fits this description?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the anatomical location, Brodmann numbers (where indicated), interconnections, and function of somatosensory and motor cortical areas: primary somatosensory, somatosensory association, primary motor, premotor, supplementary motor, parietal association

options.text: Secondary somatosensory cortex in parietal operculum
options.rationale: Correct because S2 in the parietal operculum contains a mirrored somatotopic map, bilateral receptive fields, and projects to limbic structures as noted in the lecture.

options.text: Primary somatosensory cortex on postcentral gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because S1 has unilateral precise somatotopy and receives thalamic VPL/VPM inputs; if receptive fields were strictly contralateral and highly topographic, S1 would be implicated.

options.text: Somatosensory association cortex on superior parietal lobule
options.rationale: Wrong because somatosensory association cortex synthesizes multimodal inputs for stereognosis and spatial reasoning rather than bilateral mirrored receptive fields projecting to limbic areas.

options.text: Primary motor cortex on precentral gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because M1 encodes motor goals and contains Betz cells rather than bilateral somatosensory receptive fields; if stimulation produced movement rather than sensory responses, M1 would be implicated.

options.text: Inferior parietal lobule including angular gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because inferior parietal lobule is heteromodal and involved in conceptual spatial tasks and neglect rather than mirrored bilateral somatosensory mapping projecting to limbic structures.

question: A patient with a lesion that combines proprioceptive and cutaneous streams would most likely have damage to which Brodmann area that integrates these inputs?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the anatomical location, Brodmann numbers (where indicated), interconnections, and function of somatosensory and motor cortical areas: primary somatosensory, somatosensory association, primary motor, premotor, supplementary motor, parietal association

options.text: Brodmann area 2 on postcentral gyrus
options.rationale: Correct because BA2 integrates proprioceptive input from 3a with cutaneous input from 3b and 1, combining streams for higher order somatosensory processing as described in the lecture.

options.text: Brodmann area 3b on postcentral gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because BA3b primarily processes cutaneous stimuli and receives the majority of thalamic somatosensory input; if only cutaneous sensation were affected, BA3b would be implicated.

options.text: Brodmann area 3a on postcentral gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because BA3a mainly responds to proprioceptive inputs; if only joint position sense were impaired, BA3a would be more likely.

options.text: Brodmann area 1 on postcentral gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because BA1 is primarily cutaneous and refines tactile information; if texture discrimination alone were lost, BA1 would be implicated.

options.text: Brodmann area 5 on superior parietal lobule
options.rationale: Wrong because BA5 is somatosensory association cortex synthesizing multimodal inputs rather than the primary integrative BA2; if stereognosis and spatial tasks were impaired, BA5 would be considered.

question: A patient shows inability to pantomime tool use despite intact comprehension and strength. Which deficit and cortical region pair best matches this presentation?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize symptoms of cortical sensory loss (tactile agnosia, agraphesthesia, extinction), upper motor neuron injury, apraxia (ideomotor & ideational), and forms of hemineglect. Associate these presentations with locations of cortical injury or dysfunction.

options.text: Ideomotor apraxia from left premotor or parietal damage
options.rationale: Correct because ideomotor apraxia—impaired gesture and pantomime with preserved comprehension and strength—is commonly due to premotor or posterior parietal cortical lesions as noted in the lecture.

options.text: Ideational apraxia from right frontal lobe lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because ideational apraxia affects multistep sequencing and is more associated with dominant parietal damage; if multistep task failure occurred, ideational apraxia would be likely.

options.text: Primary motor weakness from left precentral lesion
options.rationale: Wrong because primary motor lesions produce paresis and UMN signs rather than isolated pantomime inability; if strength were reduced, primary motor cortex would be implicated.

options.text: Sensory ataxia from dorsal column dysfunction
options.rationale: Wrong because sensory ataxia causes coordination problems due to proprioceptive loss rather than isolated pantomime deficits; if joint position sense were absent, dorsal column disease would fit.

options.text: Global aphasia from dominant temporal lobe infarct
options.rationale: Wrong because global aphasia impairs language comprehension and expression, not isolated pantomime with preserved comprehension; if language comprehension were lost, aphasia would be the correct diagnosis.

question: Which cortical area provides approximately half of corticospinal tract neurons including large Betz cells that influence distal upper extremity control?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the anatomical location, Brodmann numbers (where indicated), interconnections, and function of somatosensory and motor cortical areas: primary somatosensory, somatosensory association, primary motor, premotor, supplementary motor, parietal association

options.text: Primary motor cortex on precentral gyrus
options.rationale: Correct because M1 contributes roughly 50% of corticospinal neurons and contains large Betz cells that make direct inputs to distal upper extremity alpha motor neurons, per the lecture.

options.text: Premotor cortex on lateral frontal gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because premotor cortex provides about 30% of corticospinal fibers and is more involved in movement selection; if external cue-driven movement selection were impaired, premotor cortex would be implicated.

options.text: Supplementary motor area on superior frontal gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because SMA is important for self-initiated sequences and contributes fewer direct corticospinal fibers; if self-initiated sequence deficits predominated, SMA would be considered.

options.text: Primary somatosensory cortex on postcentral gyrus
options.rationale: Wrong because S1 is sensory and not a major source of corticospinal output; if sensory discrimination deficits were primary, S1 would be implicated.

options.text: Secondary somatosensory cortex in parietal operculum
options.rationale: Wrong because S2 integrates somatosensory information and projects to limbic structures rather than providing major corticospinal output; if bilateral somatosensory integration were the issue, S2 would fit.

question: A patient with a lesion in the non-dominant parietal association cortex shows persistent inattention to the left side of space. Which explanation best fits this pattern?
cited_learning_objective: Parietal association cortex is centered along the intraparietal sulcus

options.text: Right parietal association damage causing left hemineglect
options.rationale: Correct because the non-dominant (usually right) parietal association complex attends to both sides of space, and its lesion produces prominent, lasting left-sided neglect as described in the lecture.

options.text: Left parietal association damage causing left hemineglect
options.rationale: Wrong because dominant (left) parietal lesions typically cause milder, transient attentional deficits; if the lesion were right-sided, more severe neglect would be expected.

options.text: Right primary visual cortex lesion causing left neglect
options.rationale: Wrong because V1 lesions produce contralateral visual field loss rather than the multimodal attentional neglect seen with parietal association damage; if a homonymous hemianopia were present, V1 would be implicated.

options.text: Left frontal eye field lesion causing left neglect
options.rationale: Wrong because frontal eye field lesions affect gaze preference and saccades rather than sustained spatial attention; if gaze deviation toward the lesion occurred, FEF involvement would be likely.

options.text: Bilateral vestibular dysfunction causing left neglect
options.rationale: Wrong because vestibular dysfunction causes balance and spatial orientation problems rather than unilateral neglect; if vertigo and imbalance predominated, vestibular causes would be considered.

question: Which Brodmann areas form the primary somatosensory cortex and include a subdivision that receives the majority of thalamic somatosensory input?
cited_learning_objective: Recall the anatomical location, Brodmann numbers (where indicated), interconnections, and function of somatosensory and motor cortical areas: primary somatosensory, somatosensory association, primary motor, premotor, supplementary motor, parietal association

options.text: Brodmann areas 3a, 3b, 1, and 2
options.rationale: Correct because S1 comprises BA3a, 3b, 1, and 2, with BA3b having a prominent layer 4 and receiving the majority of thalamic somatosensory inputs (VPL/VPM) as stated in the lecture.

options.text: Brodmann areas 4, 5, 7, and 6
options.rationale: Wrong because BA4 is primary motor and BA5/7 are somatosensory association areas; if motor output and association functions were the focus, this grouping would be relevant.

options.text: Brodmann areas 17, 18, 19, and 20
options.rationale: Wrong because these areas are visual cortex regions (V1 and extrastriate) rather than somatosensory cortex; if visual processing deficits were present, these areas would be implicated.

options.text: Brodmann areas 1, 2, 3, and 4
options.rationale: Wrong because BA4 is motor cortex not part of S1; if motor deficits predominated with sensory loss, inclusion of BA4 would be inappropriate.

options.text: Brodmann areas 5, 7, 39, and 40
options.rationale: Wrong because these are parietal association and inferior parietal lobule areas involved in multimodal integration and neglect rather than primary somatosensory thalamic input.
